transcript.transcript: Are two heavy compared to some of the antique guns that will be on display at the Historical Museum? Yeah. I have a rusty Model 1 Smith & Wesson. And they've got one that is damaged, but it's not rusty. And I've looked at it. It's a very rare gun. You find them where you do it. The only good one I ever saw belonged to Bill Harris when he had a Western museum kind of hidden out by the car museum that he had in Reno. And I finally saw that. But Somebody in Boise put me onto it. I think somebody at the museum said, well, I know where there's a good one. Go look at that. And I did. Now, it's been a real resource in my life. When I was a kid, this park was it. Of course, my dad was a native and grew up going to the park. And he'd say, well, you want to do that? Go to the park. We'd play some kind of ball and we'd go down there. Places like where the Rose Garden is now, that was just an open field. There were lots of places for kids to play. You could go, How old were you when your parents would let you just go out alone? My parents both worked every day, all day long. And everybody else in the neighborhood did, too, just about. And when we were kids, and I grew up with a family called the Barnes's, when my folks weren't home, when I got out of school, I went to Barnes's. That was where I was supposed to go. And In the daytime, older brother Jimmy had the responsibility of keeping us out of jail. But the world was different. There weren't people trying to rape us or whatever. And so we roamed. The park was just a mile from home. And a mile wasn't very far in those days for hoofing it. Nowadays, we don't walk very far, but We did then. So I was allowed to go there. My parents trusted me. From the time you were maybe six, seven, eight? Yeah, I think maybe 10. Because When you're six, seven, or eight, you're too little. You're intimidated by big things and distance. But later, I was free to go range. Well, the foothill from our house was about three miles. And by the time I was coming into my teens, maybe 10 or 11, I had a horse that I owned that was my horse. And I was free to ride it to the foothills or ride my bike over there, just as long as I was back home at 6 o'clock a reasonable time. And I guess they didn't worry too much about me. I don't know. Could you take your horse to the park? No. No? OK. I never took the horse to the park, but I took it to Military Reserve Park, which was not a park at the time. But that's where I was headed, either there or up the Rocky Canyon Road. And it was tricky. Getting across the river, you couldn't cross with most horses. You couldn't cross and ride your horse across the Broadway Bridge or the Bovard Bridge or the Ninth Street Bridge, because the horse would shy on you and fall down. So you had to develop a route. Well, there was a route. You could enter the river just west on the south side of the river at the Broadway Bridge. And you could cross under the bridge and come up the bank on the East side of the bridge and then go right up Broadway. It worked fine. All of us did it. Lots of kids had horses in those days, and so we did. I'd go over and ride in Rocky Canyon. And, you know, didn't think much about it. It wasn't too hazardous. I'd ridden one of the horses I had. I'd ridden it. I bought it out at Mayfield, out on the mountain home desert, and rode it to Boise all by myself. I made it alive. I broke a stirrup. I was having a hell of a time. But my folks realized that, well, he did that. I guess he can do just about what he said he can do. But that whole area was just a wonderful place for kids, young people. And there was never any real problems. I don't know if it speaks well of Boise law enforcement or whatever. I never had any problems. And It wasn't because I was so well behaved or smart. It was because there was probably a lot of good things going on. Yeah, plus I think somebody had an eye on us. It was a wonderful place and the thing that I cherish a lot of memories, particularly of the ice skating. That was just pretty exceptional. How deep was the pool, the pond? It had been deliberately filled so that there was no place that was more than three feet deep. I broke through a time or two and I never got any water any higher than my knee. I think it was probably, I should ask my dad, I think it was probably a gravel pit that was then partly filled. I don't know that. See, Cottonwood Creek spills into the river at the east end of the park. But I think maybe it ran through that and came out over by where the playground was. When I was a kid, that's where it was spilling. I didn't deliberately go trace that out, but I remember all the signs of it being there, and I think it was. The other thing is that I spent 35 years with the Bell System and through a good training And through a good training and I spent the last 25 years in the marketing, business marketing. And this was a good market. And I succeeded in it. And we would get people from all over the country, all over the Bell System, coming out here to see how we did it. They'd come and it was a call to review. The headquarters of the Bell System in those days was at 195 Broadway in New York. And these big shots would come out here to see how Ralphie Joel was doing this. And I'd always take them to Julia Davis Park, because they did want to see the community, too. And it would just knock their socks off. They'd never seen anything quite like that. And my crowning thing, to leave them with a good impression, was I'd stand there on the north side of the river in my suit and black shoes and so forth, necktie, and catch a trout out of the river. It's much better than winter. Well, no. That would be summer. In the summer, well, not during high water. But the rest of the time, that stretch along there, there's a bunch of willows. It's loaded with trout. See the heavy flow of the rivers on the north side. And you just cast out into that ripple and let it drift back into the fast water. Bang, you've got a fish. And I do that with these guys watching me. You know, they're big shots at AT&T. God, I got a lot of mileage out of that. Probably a lot of raises out of it. But, you know, The only place I ever saw that kind of reminded me of that, and it's on a much smaller scale, is Colorado Springs has got a little green belt kind of in the middle of town that goes north out of there. You may have seen it sometime. And they kind of copied some of the, they didn't copy, but you know, they were similar. And it's just a wonderful place. I used to have to go to Colorado Springs once in a while for some training. And I'd always work it out so that I could go visit this green belt, which was unhandy because the place that I went was over on the side of the valley there. I can't remember the name of it now, but it was a big resort. I was always impressed with that. And I thought, well, they had to come to Boise, Idaho to see how to do this. Did you help at all when they built the green belt? Oh, sure. Yeah, you bet. My Kiwanis Club built some sections of it on our own. They laid it out, gave us a blueprint, and we built it. That's from Americana Boulevard east on the north side of the river. There's a big, long stretch there that we built. So is that part that goes through Julia Davis? No. No. OK. There wasn't anything much built in Julie Davis. It was already there. It was, yeah. But it was, you couldn't see the river as much, could you? I understood it was pretty brushy. That's right. You'd see a little bit more river now in places. Yes, much more open. Well, that's been one of the strong anchors of Boise City. Having that beautiful open land so close to the city center, I don't know how you'd calculate just how important that is and has been. Great place. Anything I haven't asked? I don't know. Yeah, it's a great place. I'd like to have a nickel for every hour I've spent there. Do you have any pictures of yourself there? Probably not. No. I don't know of any. OK. It's probably in the Augusta. I've got the Kiwanis collection, but it's not on the catalog. There may be some pictures in that. Well, if you ever come across them, let us know. I've got to unload them on whoever's in charge of history in the club now. I've got them stored here, and I don't want them. There's a lot of pictures. That sort of thing is always hard to find, Because those kind of assets always end up in the hands of somebody that doesn't know who the pictures are. That was one of the reasons that I hung on to them. But I'm not going to do anything with them. So yeah. Yeah, we've done a lot of work down there at the club. The wading pool was never used. We spent a lot of money on it. I forget whose idea it was. Somebody in the park department and one of our members, who's now in a nursing home but still alive. He says that's probably something that Gordon Bowen wanted. I don't know that, though. Well, she's got a great perk department, thanks to, among others, Gordon Bowen. People like Alice Dieter, she's kind of hard to deal with sometimes. But she held out for a lot of things that, and Stan Burns, some of the people that served on park boards and so on, they kind of knew what they wanted. I'll never forget one time Alice came to me and said, Ralph, get us some public telephones in the park. And I said, Alice, and her husband was the district engineer that I had to deal with all the time. I said, the reason we don't have more telephones in the park is nobody wants to put any money in them. And we've got to at least break even on them, you know, if we're going to leave them there. And if they don't get used a lot, they don't work. You've got to use them, or they'll get so they won't work. And they get vandalized. Well, she said, my god, I'm going to see that you put some phones in there. And I said, well, I'll work out a proposal for you guys. I'll do that. And I did it myself, and I went and made a presentation to the park board and it it kind of balanced what I did is I studied the parks carefully and found some locations that the public would see the thing and use them. And I insisted on having adequate signing on them so that they could see them, etc. Etc. Well of course Alice and the park board didn't want to do that. I said, well, fair's fair. You come around part way at least, they're my view, maybe we can put in a few. And we did, but they're not a good location. Are they still there? No. No, you don't. Well, of course, cell phones have replaced a lot of the coin phones. But you've got to be able to depend on them If somebody wants to use one in an emergency, then the damn thing won't work, because it's only been used three times in the last month. Yeah. You haven't gained anything. Plus, you've spent $1, 000 putting it in there, making it work, and so forth, at least the first time. So We can never work that out very well. There's some other technologies that could be used for something like that. I don't imagine there's anybody interested in that anymore. What about homeless people? When you were on the city council and on the park board, was that a problem? Sometimes. It wasn't a high priority problem, but yes. In fact, I went down there one time while I was on the council just to check it out for myself. It scared me to death. But I think they cleared that out. I probably told a full run about it at the time, or somebody that I knew, you know, that wouldn't make a big flap out of it. Run to the newspapers. He can't help but feel sorry for homeless people, but that's a poor place for him to go. You're going to restrict him from this place, that place, and the other. You need to also do something positive and find a place for them to go all at the same time. I was always aware of that, but a lot of people weren't. Yeah, right. But that wasn't a high priority problem while I was on the council. It came up. And vandalism, that's one of the, I don't know anything about current vandalism, but I always had the impression that Julia Davis is not high on vandalism. It seems like the parks you're out a little ways get beat up more. I know I don't go to Camel's Back much because this dog I've got, there's something over there that scares her so I've just never been able to take her over there. She gets so crazy that it's not worth it. But it used to be this time of the year, every time they close up the bathrooms over there, somebody smashed the doors in. That camel sack. Yeah, break up the equipment and so forth. Some teenagers or whatever they were. I don't know what moves people to do things like that, but something does. Because you do it over and over again. I was out Saturday morning with some of my Kewanian buddies, and we were cleaning the first two miles of the Bogus Basin Road. And one of the things I found was part of somebody's toilet. It's smashed up and thrown out there. Oh, dear. Hard to carry one of those in an orange bag. Yeah, right. People are crazy. You're pretty sheltered from that in your gated community now. Right. Right. Yeah. I don't have any trouble. We don't have much trouble here in the North End. Uh-huh. You know, I've seen some neighborhoods, you know, look out in your front yard and somebody's thrown something like that in here. People are nuts. And there, you know, the history of Julia Davis, they found, I remember, those two buildings. The, well, I don't remember the name of the art gallery, but the museum was a WPA building, or part of it was. And I wouldn't be surprised what something of the art gallery was, too. Well, that was in the years when I was going to high school. Somebody found a body there where the museum is. It was quite a long running story. They finally figured out who did it, and who was, and who killed him, and so on. But that was a big, big flap for a couple of years, trying to get that resolved. That building stood partly completed for a long time. And there was a big trench around it. And I'm thinking that somebody found a body in there. One of our local kids. Kids was killed by some thug. Huh. OK, We don't have to keep going on and on. You've told me all you want to say. Well, I can't think of anything else. OK. You have very, very great memories. Wonderful. Oh, it's a great place. Well, it was just off the edge of the yard, sort of. Well, this is wonderful. Thank you. I was used to great, you talk about distance. See, when I walked to grade school, It was a mile and a quarter. I lived on the far end of Boise Avenue and the Barnes kids and I, Patsy and Arthur, walked to school together every day. All those years, only one winter in 1937, there was a hell of a snowstorm. We had to have help. The rest of the time we made it. There was no school. You went to Garfield? Yeah. Then you went to East? No. East didn't exist. I went to North. North? Now, that was a hike. You walked to North? Yeah, there wasn't any school bus. Really? You could take the city bus and get partway. Yeah, that was a tough deal. And what was even tougher about it is the kids from Garfield, up until my class went all the way to the eighth grade at Garfield. Our class didn't get to. We had to go to North. We hated it. Boy did we hate it. Yeah, that was a long way. And, you know, going through...and I walked it. Until I got old enough to drive a car. I walked, which I could do. But it was going through downtown Boise was a struggle because there was a switch yard for the railroads that were switching freight cars back and forth. So if you went through there at 8 o'clock in the morning with a white shirt on or something, why you got all that soot on you? By the time you got to school, you were dirty. Where was the switch yard? Was it between the connector and the west? Well, right down through, yeah, or Front Street, just on the south side of Front. That was the switch yard. Oh, I wanted to add, too. And again, Did your father ever tell you any stories about using the park when he was a boy? Because your dad was born here too? Yeah. We'll see now. You know, he grew up over in what is now Ann Morrison Park. That's where they live. So that was really close. Yeah. But he learned how to trap and fish and all that. And except for the skating on the pond, I don't know. I never did. I never came up. I don't know. Now, he skated there, of course. Yeah. As a boy? Yeah. Yeah, that was the best place in town to skate. The safest. And he was a much better skater than I ever was. I've got his skates. Do you? Yeah. Great. They're not in very good shape, but I've got them. And he could do a lot of tricks and things like that that I never got as good. I could do them, but I generally fell down. Yeah. But he was, you know, he had his own trap line and things like that. And I think he was less apt to go to a park for recreation than I was. He was pretty much of a loner. He had some buddies that he hung out with, but they went to the mountains and so forth. Not that I didn't do that, too. But, yeah. I think kids were a lot more on their own in his time and even at my time. Uh-huh. I don't know that that was good, but it was a fact of life. Uh-huh. Go out and play until dinner time. Well, you know, people worked 12 hours a day. My folks often worked long shifts, particularly my dad. He, after his dad split their business, why, He ran a couple of, at different times, 24-hour service places downtown. One of them was called, I think they called it the service tire. He was the manager there and worked the shift. And then Walter Franes, who was the Chrysler plant dealer, built a big service station. And my dad managed that. And those guys, they worked long shifts and seven days a week sometimes. I don't think he ever worked seven days a week, but when he wasn't working, he was fishing. He was gone. And I didn't go with him very much until I got old enough to drive a car. Not old enough to be a licensed driver, but old enough to drive it. Then he would get out and fish down. He liked to fish the main stem of the Boise, Middleport. And he'd fish down the river. And I'd drive the car to where he got out. I knew where he would get out. I'd take a book, and I'd drive the car. When I was just a little kid, I had a hell of a time sitting over the dashboard. But I'd just drive it in one gear. And we'd get along fine. He was too competitive to let me fish with him. He's the guy who wanted the best and the biggest fish. And he didn't. He wasn't very fatherly. He was a great guy, a wonderful guy, but he wasn't fatherly, which was OK. I didn't care. It didn't bother me. Looking back on it, I know that we could probably get it done a little bit more. A little more things, right. Yeah. Well, I want to thank you very much, Officially! Very nice. Thank you. Okay? So, that's it for this video. I hope you enjoyed it. And if you did, please leave a like. And if you want to see more videos like this, please subscribe to my channel. And I'll see you in the next video. Bye!

lines.line: # Antique Guns and Historical Museum
